The Art of Sequencing (Completed 05/07/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 1 of 2
Speaker 1: Hey guys, welcome back to Lesson Three of Module One. I just want to take a quick
second and introduce you to a term called: Engineering The Conversion Context. What
this really is, is it's sequencing. This is going to be a short lesson, I just want us to get
very familiar with the terminology that we're going to be using from this point out.
Now, a lot of people always ask me how I sequence out these crazy long campaigns. On
the surface, they see these 15, 20, 30, 40 sometimes even 50 email sequences and they
freak out. They're like, "I can never write something like that." But what they don't see
is that I never actually sequence more than three or five emails in a row. Instead, there
are these multiple sub-narratives, each one with their own goal.
So just like how a coach or a skilled therapist who is trying facilitate a transformation for
their client would need these parameters or boundaries to do it. Maybe several sessions
each with their own theme or their own goal, the same really goes for our campaigns.
So instead of one, crazy long sequence, we're actually creating these multiple sub-
campaigns or conversion contexts that give the right focused attention to the beliefs
and understandings that we're trying to instill or coach at that moment.
The big point here is that there is actually no such thing as a "general product launch
campaign". So you can kind of drop the anxiety around that. Drop the anxiety around
having to open up a Google Doc and fill it with 30 or 40 new emails by the end of the
week. There actually is no such thing. Instead, there are these five core sub-sequences
that will appear across every funnel, every kind of launch campaign that you create.
In the next lesson, you're actually going to get introduced to these five main sub-
sequences in further detail, and in Modules Three through Seven, you're going to get an
even deeper dive into each of them. How they work, what we're coaching as well as the
theme plates, the go-to emails that I use to actually activate those principals or those
beliefs, or those attitudes that we are coaching. But for now, I just want you to take
home the following: Number one, don't think of your campaigns as one super long
sequence. Instead, just break them down into those sub-sequences that specifically
address the micro-conversion that needs to take place to move your prospect along the
buyer's journey.
Then the last one is important. As our tools get more and more sophisticated, our CRMs,
our email service providers are able to kind of really cap into so many different
behavioral triggers, and it becomes really tempting to play around with that and
leverage all of the things that our tools can do. But I'm really going to ask you to just
focus on these five core sub-sequences that we're going to get to throughout this
course, and then get fancy later once you have those dialed in. To help you get fancy,
once you get there, in the bonus section you will learn a little concept I call:
"Contextology". That's how you can create more conversion context or profit mines, as I
like to call them, within your funnel.
Now these will be based on the behavioral triggers that your software flags down.
Whether people are opening, not opening, registering, not registering. These are the
things that you're going to leverage through a concept I call "Contextology". So it is a bit
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

more advanced, and it is something that goes beyond the scope of this training, but in
that bonus, you are going to get the essentials, at least the skills to uncover those profit
mines as well as the few that I use in my own campaigns and campaigns for clients that
really, really make the biggest impact on the bottom line.
So that's it for now. I want you to take a breath, soak this in, and when you're ready,
come meet me at the next lesson where we're going to get a quicker overview of each
of those five core sub-sequences. I'll see you there.
